title: The K distribution
text: Suppose X∼χm2X \sim \chi^2_m Chi-squared random variate mm degrees freedom. distribution Y=XmY = \sqrt{\frac{X}{m}} Kay distribution mm degrees freedom, written Y∼Km.Y \sim K_m. density f(y)={mm2ym−1e−12my22m2−1Γ(m2)for0≤y<∞0otherwise.f(y) = \left\{\begin{array}{lcl}     \frac{m^{\frac{m}{2}} y ^{m-1} e^{-\frac{1}{2} m y^2}} {2^{\frac{m}{2}-1} \Gamma(\frac{m}{2})} &~~~& \text{} ~~ 0 \le y < \infty \\     &&\\     0 && \text{otherwise}.     \end{array}     \right. KmK_m density attractive features χm2\chi^2_m density: KmK_m much symmetric density χm2\chi^2_m, mm; like χm2\chi^2_m density KmK_m density, m→∞m \rightarrow \inftyKmK_m becomes symmetric nearly normally distributed faster χm2\chi^2_m; m↑m \uparrow, KmK_m density concentrates around value y=1y=1, rather heading ∞\infty like χm2\chi^2_m; m increases, Km better properties values calculated using dkay(...) density function. example, dkay(1.0, df=10) = 1.7546737.

title: The K distribution
text: Perhaps obvious relation normal random variate KmK_m Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim N(0,1), |Z|∼K1|Z|\sim K_1, half-normal. important applications distribution estimator sample standard deviation proportional KmK_m. precise, Y1,…,YnY_1, \ldots, Y_n independent identically distributed N(μ,σ2)N(\mu, \sigma^2) random variates, realizations y1,…,yny_1, \ldots, y_n usual estimates μ̂=∑yi/n\widehat{\mu} = \sum y_i /n σ̂=∑(yi−μ̂)2/(n−1)\widehat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\sum (y_i - \widehat{\mu})^2/(n-1)}, corresponding estimators μ̃\widetilde{\mu} σ̃\widetilde{\sigma} distributed μ̃∼N(μ,σ2n)andσ̃σ∼Kn−1. \widetilde{\mu} \sim N(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n})  ~~~~~\text{} ~~~~~  \frac{\widetilde{\sigma}}{\sigma} \sim K_{n-1}.  latter shows KmK_m used inference (e.g. tests confidence intervals) σ\sigma. handy KmK_m quantiles vary much less χm2\chi^2_m. example, condider following table cumulative distribution. Unlike χm2\chi^2_m distribution, quantiles table stabilize, allowing 1±0.201 \pm 0.20 bad rule thumb 90%90\% probability ratio σ̃/σ\widetilde{\sigma}/\sigma. values calculated using qkay(...) quantile function. example, qkay(0.05, df=5) = 0.478639. used construct interval estimates σ\sigma. get observed significance levels, cumulative distribution function pkay(...) used. example, SL = 1- pkay(1.4, df=10) = 1 - 0.9667287 = 0.0332713.

title: The K distribution
text: standard normal theory, Student tmt_m distribution can defined follows. Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim N(0,1) Y∼KmY \sim K_m distributed independently ZZ, ratio T=ZY=N(0,1)Km=tmT=\frac{Z}{Y} = \frac{N(0,1)}{K_m} = t_m fairly easy remember. estimators model μ̃−μσ̃/n=μ̃−μσ/nσ̃σ=N(0,1)Kn−1=tn−1\frac{\widetilde{\mu} - \mu}        {\widetilde{\sigma} / \sqrt{n}}        = \frac{ \frac{\widetilde{\mu}-\mu}                      {\sigma/\sqrt{n}}                      }               {\frac{\widetilde{\sigma}}                     {\sigma}                     }                = \frac{N(0,1)}{K_{n-1}} = t_{n-1}  used construct interval estimates tests value parameter μ\mu.

title: The K distribution
text: every distribution R four functions provided KmK_m distribution. dkay(x, df=m, ...) evalutes density KmK_m xx, pkay(x, df=m, ...) evalutes distribution KmK_m xx, qkay(p, df=m, ...) evalutes quantile KmK_m proportion pp, rkay(n, df=m, ...) generates nn pseudo-random realizations KmK_m. parameters ellipsis include non-centrality parameter. functions rely corresponding χm2\chi^2_m functions base R. briefly illustrate .

title: The K distribution
code: x <- seq(0,2,0.01) plot(x, dkay(x, df=10), type="l", col="steelblue",       main="Density", xlab="x", ylab="f(x)") abline(v=1.0, lty=2, col="grey")

what: The cumulative distribution function pkay(x, df, ...)
title: The K distribution
code: x <- seq(0,2,0.01) plot(x, pkay(x, df=10), type="l", col="steelblue",       main="Distribution", xlab="x", ylab="F(x)") abline(v=1.0, lty=2, col="grey")

title: The K distribution
code: x <- seq(0,2,0.01) p <- pkay(x, df=10) plot(p, qkay(p, df=10), type="l", col="steelblue",       main="Quantile function", xlab="p", ylab="Q(p)") abline(h=1.0, lty=2, col="grey")

title: The K distribution
code: x <- rkay(1000, df=10) hist(x, col="steelblue",       main="Pseudo-random numbers", xlab="x") abline(v=1.0, lty=2, col="grey")

title: Self Calibrating Quantile-Quantile Plots for Visual Testing
text: Quantile–quantile plots, qqplots, important visual tool many applications interpretation requires care often experience. apparent subjectivity unnecessary. drawing computational display facilities now widely available, qqplots easily enriched help interpretation. new enhanced qqplot, self-calibrating qqplot, introduced demonstrated variety examples (examples original paper appear help/reference page qqtest). Possibilities endless. standard examples include normal qqplots, log-normal plots, half-normal plots (e.g. factorial experiments), comparison empirical distributions. Self-calibration visually incorporating sampling variation qqplot display variety ways. qqtest draws quantile quantile plot visually assessing whether data come test distribution defined one many ways. vertical axis plots data quantiles, horizontal test distribution. default behaviour generates 1000 samples test distribution overlays plot shaded pointwise interval estimates ordered quantiles test distribution. small number independently generated exemplar quantile plots can also overlaid. interval estimates exemplars provide different comparative information assess evidence provided qqplot hypothesis data come test distribution (default normal gaussian). Finally, visual test significance (lineup plot) can also displayed test null hypothesis data come test distribution. self-calibrating plot can created distribution whose quantile function can provided user, making useful wide variety applications. plots particularly helpful students gaining experience interpreting quantile-quantile plots, instructors hoping teach basic understanding interpretation quantile-quantile plots. Please read reference explanation historical development quantile-quantile plot.

title: Bacteria from Delaware River water entering the Torresdale Filter of the Philadelphia water supply 1913. — bacteria

title: Bacteria from Delaware River water entering the Torresdale Filter of the Philadelphia water supply 1913. — bacteria
text: Rather individual daily results, recorded 22 values together percentage days whose value less equal recorded value. quantiles therefore based 365 daily measurements. Values taken logarithmic-normal probability plot dated January 21, 1915 appeared Figure 22 George C. Whipple's 1916 (Part 2) paper “Element Chance Sanitation”. paper introduces logarithimic-normal probability paper (log-normal qqplot). (bacteria, plot(qnorm(percentTime/100), log(count,10), type="o")) reproduces Whipple's 1915 plot. (bacteria, qqtest(data=count, p=percentTime/100, np=365, dist="log-normal", type="o"))  effect qqtest plot data.  detail can : (bacteria, qqtest(data=log(count, 10), p=percentTime/100, np=365, dist="normal", type="o"))

code: pkay(1, 20) #> [1] 0.5420703 q <- seq(0.01, 1.8, 0.01) # # Plot the cdf for K(5) u <- pkay(q,5) plot(q, u, type="l",      xlab="q", ylab="cumulative probability",      xlim=range(q), ylim=c(0,1),      main="K cdf") # # Add some other K cdfs lines(q, pkay(q,10), lty=2) lines(q, pkay(q,20), lty=3) lines(q, pkay(q,30), lty=4) legend("topleft",        legend=c("df = 5", "df = 10", "df = 20", "df = 30"),        lty=c(1,2,3,4),        title="degrees of freedom",        cex=0.75, bty="n")   # # See the vignette for more on the "K-distribution" #

title: qkay The K distribution quantile function — qkay
code: p <- ppoints(30) # Get the quantiles for these points q5 <- qkay(p, 5) plot(p, q5, main="Quantile plot of K(20)", ylim=c(0,max(q5))) # Add quantiles from another K points(p, qkay(p, 20), pch=19)   # # Do these EXACT quantiles from a K(5) look like they might # have been generated from K(20)? qqtest(q5, dist="kay",df=20)   # How about compared to normal? qqnorm(q5)  qqtest(q5)  # for this many degrees of freedom it looks a lot like # a gaussian (normal) distribution  # And should look really good compared to the true distribution qqtest(q5, dist="kay", df=5)  # # # But not so much like it came from a K on 1 degree of freedom qqtest(q5, dist="kay",df=1)   # # See the vignette for more on the "K-distribution" #

what: qqtest A self-calibrated quantile-quantile plot for assessing distributional shape. — qqtest
title: qqtest A self-calibrated quantile-quantile plot for assessing distributional shape. — qqtest
text: Draws quantile-quantile plot visually assessing whether data come    test distribution defined one many ways. vertical axis plots data quantiles, horizontal test distribution. Interval estimates exemplars provide different comparative information assess    evidence provided qqplot hypothesis data come test distribution    (default normal gaussian).    Interval estimates provide test information related individual quantiles,    exemplars provide test information related shape quantile quantile curve. Optionally, visual test significance (lineup plot) can displayed provide    coarse level significance testing null hypothesis data come    test distribution. default behaviour generates 1000 samples test distribution overlays    plot pointwise interval estimates ordered quantiles test distribution. Various option choices available effect different visualizations    uncertainty surrounding quantile quantile plot.    include overlaying independently generated exemplar test distribution sample    quantile traces assess joint (opposed pointwise) distribution quantiles. See argument descriptions examples details.

title: qqtest A self-calibrated quantile-quantile plot for assessing distributional shape. — qqtest
text: data univariate dataset tested. data one column, first used. dist name distribution comparison made, test distribution built-distributions.   One "gaussian" ("normal"), "log normal","half normal",   "uniform","exponential","student", "chi-squared", "kay".   first three characters needed specify dist. dist "student",  "chi-squared", "kay", value degrees freedom   argument (df ) also required. df Degrees freedom dist used dist either "student" "chi-squared". qfunction non-NULL, must function single argument (proportion, p say) used calculate quantiles test distribution.  non-NULL, rfunction also non-NULL. value dist argument ignored case. rfunction non-NULL, must function single argument (count, n say) used randomly select sample size n test distribution. non-NULL, qfunction must also non-NULL. qfunction non-NULL  rfunction NULL, qfunction applied output call runif place NULL rfunction  (.e. probability integral transform used generate random sample). value dist argument ignored whenever qfunction function. dataTest non-NULL, must second data set. empirical distribution given data used test distribution value data tested. non-NULL, values arguments dist, qfunction, rfunction ignored favour using empirical distribution test distribution. p non-NULL, must vector containing probability points quantiles calculated. length vector data, p taken probabilities correspond data; otherwise data taken provide empirical quantile function values taken p produce plot. p NULL (default), p determined function ppoints(n,) n number data points data given argument . second parameter given ppoints call determine p necessary. NULL, default values chosen specific distribution (e.g. 3/8 gaussian, 0 uniform, etc.) 2/5 otherwise, following recommendations C. Cunnane (1978). Users may supply value original Hazen's 1/2 Tukey's 1/3. np required vector p provided. p take values, need know np sample size used  construct quantiles provided vlaues p. p np provided simulation based simulating values distribution order statistics p*np. matchMethod necessary match locations scales two distributions.  method used matching given matchMethod must one c("hinges","quartiles", "middlehalf", "bottomhalf", "tophalf"). line fitted data coefficients used adjust location scale test quantiles match data. matchMethod = "hinges" (default), line fit (x, y) pairs given three central values fivenum() coordinates; "quartiles" three quartiles coordinate paired line fit. method "middlehalf", name suggests, fits line middle half sorted data.  way, similar previous two methods.  three fairly robust use central part data estimate location scale based roughly matching medians inter-quartile spreads. remaining two methods, "bottomhalf" "tophalf", use line fitted bottom top half sorted data. generally recommended might used comparison dist skewed.  example chi-squared distribution, one might use bottomhalf match data shorter left tail distribution.  Similarly, "tophalf" might preferred dist skewed opposite direction. Finally, note user may supply matching method providing function vector arguments x y horizontal vertical quantiles returns vector coefficients (intercept, slope) line used match quantile location scales. xAxisAsProbs TRUE (default FALSE) horizontal axis labelled probabilities. cumulative probabilities according test distribution.  located corresponding quantile values. handy comparing percentiles test data distributions well giving measure symmetry tail weights test distribution location. FALSE axis labelled according quantile values. yAxisAsProbs TRUE (default FALSE) vertical axis labelled probabilities. cumulative probabilities according empirical distribution data. located corresponding quantile values. handy comparing percentiles test data distributions well giving measure symmetry tail weights data distribution location. FALSE axis labelled according quantile values. xAxisProbs vector probabilities used label x axis ticks xAxisAsProbs TRUE. Default c(0.05, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.95).  Ignored xAxisAsProbs FALSE. yAxisProbs vector probabilities used label y axis ticks yAxisAsProbs TRUE. Default c(0.05, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.95).  Ignored yAxisAsProbs FALSE. nreps number replicate samples taken test distribution construct pointwise intervals quantile.  Default 1000. samples, empirical distribution generated test distribution ordered quantiles corresponding values ofppoints(length(data)). used construct central intervals whatever proportions given centralPercents. centralPercents vector proportions determining central intervals empirical distribution ordered quantile test distribution. Default c(0.90, 0.95, 0.99) corresponding central 90, 95, 99% simulated pointwise confidence intervals quantile coming test distribution sample size data. quality interval locations typically increases nreps decreases probability used interval. envelope TRUE (default), grey envelope plotted showing central intervals quantile shade grey.  higher corresponding probability associated interval, lighter shade. outermost edges envelope range simulated data test distribution. envelope thus provides (pointwise) density estimate quantiles drawn test distribution sample size. FALSE envelope drawn. drawPercentiles TRUE, pair curves plotted show central intervals different line type. plotted envelope envelope TRUE. FALSE (default) simulated percentile curves drawn. drawQuartiles TRUE, pair curves plotted show quartiles (central 50% region) ordered quantiles simulated test distribution.  median also plotted solid line type. plotted envelope envelope TRUE. FALSE (default) none curves drawn. legend TRUE (default NULL) nreps>0 legend appearance simulated ranges central intervals added plot. FALSE, legend appears. NULL, legend always appears except lineup = TRUE. legend.xy Either string one c("bottomright", "bottom", "bottomleft", "left", "topleft","top", "topright", "right", "center")  (default "topleft") list named components x y interpreted exactly function legend() graphics package. legend.cex Character expansion size legend (default 0.8). nexemplars (default 0) number replicate samples taken test distribution plotted coloured trail qqplot. trail sample size data truly coming test distribution. trail gives idea shape qqplot sample size test distribution. Together, give sense variability plot's shape. typex (default "o", match type supplied) plot type used plotting exemplars, legal values  type argument plot. plainTrails TRUE, single grey colour used exemplar trails. FALSE (default), exemplar trail shown different colour. colTrails Colours used trails (default multi-colours). alphaTrails alpha transparency used plotting exemplar trails. default 0.25. trails overplot, smaller alphaTrails value recommended nexemplars increases. lwdTrails graphical line width (lwd) used plotting exemplar trails.   default 1. trails overplot, combining larger lwdTrails envelope = FALSE, lower alphaTrails value larger nexemplars can give truer sense density qqplot configurations envelope = TRUE. lineup TRUE (default FALSE) qqplot data randomly located grid nsuspects plots.  Identical arguments given construct qqtest plots grid. Assuming viewer seen qqplot data , successful selection true data plot grid plots corresponds evidence hypothesis data come test distribution. Significance level 1/nsuspects. Similarly, viewer narrows selection k possible subjects true location among , corresponding observed level significance (p-value) k//nsuspects. plot given suspect number 1 nsuspects (left right, top bottom). suspect number plot corresponding actual data returned, slightly obfuscated help keep test honest. nsuspects total number plots (default 20) viewed lineup display lineup lineup. col non-NULL, col must colour used points plot.  NULL (default), hcl colour used values arguments h, c, l, alpha. h hue colour points.  Specified angle degrees 0 360 around colour wheel.  E.g. 0 red, 120 green, 240 blue,  Default 260 (bluish). c chroma colour points.  Takes values 0 upper bound function hue, h,  luminance, l.  Roughly, fixed h l higher value c greater intensity colour. l luminance colour points.  Takes values 0 100. given combination hue, h,  chroma, c, subset range possible.  Roughly, fixed h c higher value l lighter colour. alpha alpha transparency colour points.  Takes values 0 1. Values near 0 transparent, values near 1 (default) opaque. Alpha values sum points plot, giving indication density. cex graphical parameter cex size points. pch graphical parameter pch point character used points.  Default 19, filled circle. type graphical parameter type points qqplot. Default "p". main graphical parameter main providing title plot. NULL (default), title "qqtest" lineup = FALSE "Suspect: " followed plot number lineup = TRUE.  empty string suppress title. xlab graphical parameter xlab labelling x axis plot. NULL (default), xlab created based information available arguments qqtest test distribution.  empty string suppress labelling. ylab graphical parameter ylab labelling y axis plot. NULL (default), ylab created based information available arguments qqtest. empty string suppress labelling. xlim graphical parameter xlim determining display limits x axis. ylim graphical parameter ylim determining display limits y axis. axes graphical parameter axes determining whether axes displayed (default NULL TRUE except lineup=TRUE, axes FALSE). bty graphical parameter bty determining type box used enclose plot (default "o", set bty="n" box). ... graphical parameters passed plot function.

title: qqtest A self-calibrated quantile-quantile plot for assessing distributional shape. — qqtest
text: Displays qqplot. Invisibly returns list named components x, y, order giving horizontal vertical locations points sorted order, well order vector input data.   result qqtest must assigned get . values , example, used identify label points display. lineup TRUE, returns string encoding true location data calculation evaluated.  provides simple obfuscation true location visual assessment can honest.  true location revealed calling revealLocation() returned value.

title: qqtest A self-calibrated quantile-quantile plot for assessing distributional shape. — qqtest
code: # # default qqtest plot qqtest(precip, main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # # qqtest to compare to qqnorm op <- par(mfrow=c(1,2)) qqnorm(precip, main="qqnorm") qqtest(precip, main="qqtest",        xAxisAsProbs=FALSE, yAxisAsProbs=FALSE)  par(op) # #  Use lines instead of envelope qqtest(precip, envelope=FALSE, drawPercentiles=TRUE,        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # #  Use quartiles instead of envelope qqtest(precip, envelope=FALSE, drawQuartiles=TRUE,        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # #  Use coloured exemplars (qqplot of data simulated from the test distribution) #  and suppress the envelope.  Where the envelope, percentiles, and quartiles are #  simulated pointwise bands, exemplars give some sense of what the (joint) shape of the #  quantile-quantile plot should look like (for data from the test distribution). #  Each simulated sample is a different colour. qqtest(precip, nexemplars=10, typex="o", envelope=FALSE, type="p",        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # #  Alternatively, the trail of each exemplar could be plain (the identical grey). #  Making each trail wide and assigning it some transparency (alpha near 0) #  allows the trails to give a sense of the density through the darkness of the grey. # qqtest(precip, nexemplars=20, envelope=FALSE,        lwdTrails=3, plainTrails=TRUE, alphaTrail=0.4, typex="o", type="o",        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # #  Wide coloured exemplars with some transparency provide an indication of #  density and allow some trails to be followed by colour. # qqtest(precip, nexemplars=20, envelope=FALSE,        lwdTrails=3,  alphaTrail=0.4, typex="o", type="o", col="black",        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")    #  Envelope and exemplars with coloured trails to be followed. # qqtest(precip, nexemplars=5,        lwdTrails=2, alphaTrail=0.6, alpha=0.8,        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # # #  gaussian - qqplot, but now showing in the line up trueLoc <- qqtest(precip, lineup=TRUE, main="Suspect", legend=FALSE,                   cex=0.75, col="grey20", ylab="", pch=21)  # the location of the real data in the line up can be found by evaluating # the contents of the string trueLoc #> [1] "2^(log(471004401654115264, base=29) - 10.5)" # # Cut and paste the string contents into the R console, or simply revealLocation(trueLoc) #> [1] 3 # # # log-normal ... using the bacteria data from Whipple(1916) data(bacteria, package="qqtest") # Note that these are selected percentiles from a sample of 365 days in a year with(bacteria,     qqtest(count, dist = "log-normal", p=percentTime/100, np=365, type="o",        yAxisAsProbs=FALSE, ylab="bacteria per cc",            xAxisProbs = c(0.01, 0.50,0.75, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99, 0.995),            xlab="Percentage of days in 1913",            main = "Number of bacteria from the Delaware river in 1913")     ) ptics <- c(0.01, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 0.99 ) axis(1,at=qnorm(ptics), labels=floor(ptics*100)) yvals <- c(100, 1000, 10000, 100000) axis(2, at=log(yvals,10),      labels=c("100", "1,000", "10,000", "100,000"))  # # compare this to the log-scaled normal qqplot # # with(bacteria,     qqtest(log(count, 10), dist = "normal",            p=percentTime/100, np=365,        type="o", axes=FALSE,            ylab="bacteria per cc",            xlab="Proportion of days in 1913",            main = "Number of bacteria from the Delaware river in 1913")     )  # # # Half normal ... using the penicillin data from Daniel(1959) data(penicillin)  qqtest(penicillin, dist = "half-normal")   # Or the same again but with significant contrast labelled   with (penicillin,   {qqtest(value, yAxisProbs=c(0.1, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95),          dist="half-normal",       ylab="Sample cumulative probability",          xlab="Half-normal cumulative probability")    ppAdj <- (1+ppoints(31))/2  # to get half-normals from normal    x <- qnorm(ppAdj)    valOrder <- order(value)    # need data and rownames in increasing order    y <- value[valOrder]    tags <- rownames(penicillin)[valOrder]    selPoints <- 28:31          # going to label only the largest effects   xoffset <- c(0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.075)  # text function is a bit off    text(x[selPoints]-xoffset, y[selPoints],        tags[selPoints],        pos=2, cex=0.75)   } )   # Alternatively, use the returned results for a lot less work results <- qqtest(penicillin$value, yAxisProbs=c(0.1, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95),                      dist="half-normal",                      ylab="Sample cumulative probability",                      xlab="Half-normal cumulative probability") tags <- row.names(penicillin)[results$order] selPoints <- 28:31          # going to label only the largest effects xoffset <- c(0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.075)  # text function is a bit off text(results$x[selPoints]-xoffset, results$y[selPoints],      tags[selPoints],      pos=2, cex=0.75)   # or the same points could have been identified interactively vusing # identify(results$x, results$y, labels = row.names(penicillin)[results$order]) # # K on 9 df  ... see help(dkay) # Use data on primer paint thickness (standard deviations on n=10) data(primer, package="qqtest") with (primer,        qqtest(s,  dist="kay", df=9,             yAxisAsProbs=FALSE,           ylab="Standard deviation of primer thickness (in mils)")     )  # # chi-squared on 3 df # Use robust covariance matrix in calculation Mahalanobis distances # for the classical Brownlee stackloss data. data(stacklossDistances, package="qqtest") with(stacklossDistances,      qqtest(robust, dist="chi", df=3, ylab="Robust Mahalanobis distances"))  # # # user supplied qfunction and rfunction -- compare to beta distribution qqtest(precip,        qfunction=function(p){qbeta(p, 2, 2)},        rfunction=function(n){rbeta(n, 2, 2)},        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # # # user supplied qfunction only -- compare to beta distribution qqtest(precip,        qfunction=function(p){qbeta(p, 2, 2)},        main = "Precipitation (inches/year) in 70 US cities")  # # comparing data samples # # Does the sample of beaver2's temperatures look like they # could have come from a distribution shaped like beaver1's? #    qqtest(beaver2[,"temp"],            dataTest=beaver1[,"temp"],            ylab="Beaver 2", xlab="Beaver 1",            main="Beaver body temperatures")

what: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
text: Random generation K distribution df degrees freedom non-centrality parameter ncp. K distribution square root chi-square divided degrees freedom.  , x chi-squared m degrees freedom, y = sqrt(x/m) K m degrees freedom.    standard normal theory, K distribution pivotal quantity  s/sigma s sample standard deviation sigma standard deviation parameter normal density.  K natural distribution tests confidence intervals sigma.    K densities nearly symmetric chi-squared concentrate near 1.  degrees freedom increase, become symmetric, concentrated, nearly normally distributed.

title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
code: rkay(n, df, ncp = 0)

title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
text: n Number observations. length(n) > 1, length taken number required. df Degrees freedom (non-negative, can non-integer). ncp Non-centrality parameter (non-negative).

title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
text: rkay returns pseudo-randomly generated values. Invalid arguments result return value NaN, warning.

title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
text: Depends call analogous chi-squared functions.  See rchisq details non-centrality parameter calculations.

title: rkay The K distribution - generating pseudo-random values — rkay
code: x <- rkay(100, 20) hist(x, main="100 observations from a K(20)")  # Certainly looks like it comes from a K on 20 qqtest(x, dist="kay",df=20)  # for this many degrees of freedom it looks # a lot like a gaussian (normal) distribution qqtest(x, dist="gau",df=1)  # But not like it came from a K on 1 degree of freedom qqtest(x, dist="kay",df=1)  # # See the vignette for more on the "K-distribution" #

title: qqtest 1.2.1
text: Replaced DOI URL \doi{} markup. Corrected literal braces documentation hideLocation().

title: qqtest 1.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2020-03-16

title: qqtest 1.2.0
text: Previous releases depended upon lmRob() function robust package. package became orphaned CRAN, archived, removed. Consequently, dependency needed addressed `qqtest. done time R core policy, qqtest removed CRAN. Release 1.2.0 remedies adds functionality. now dependencies beyond base packages grDevices, graphics, stats.

title: qqtest 1.2.0
text: lineup = TRUE value returned qqtest() now just string containing obfuscated location true data. longer list single names component trueLoc.

title: qqtest 1.2.0
text: new argument matchMethod now offers several choices matching location scale data test distribution. new arguments legend.xy legend.cex let user make adjustment location character size automatically constructed legend. two new functions hideLocation() revealLocation() added use lineup = TRUE case. can also used broadly obfuscate integers range 1 n. result string evaluated reveal integer. point encryption, rather prevent easy visual decoding. lineup = TRUE, now qqtest() simply returns string true location data obfuscated. function revealLocation() return numerical location true data. Unfortunately slight increase convenience also makes slightly easier user cheat hence invalidate lineup test. (case, qqtest() used return list named component trueLoc, code depending result list need updated.) lineup = FALSE, qqtest() now invisibly returns list named components x, y, order giving horizontal vertical locations points sorted order, well order vector input data.

title: qqtest 1.1.0
text: Released CRAN April 2015, removed R core March 2020 dependency robust package orphaned archived . Functionality based R. Wayne Oldford (2016), “Self calibrating quantile-quantile plots”, American Statistician, 70, pp. 74-90. https://doi.org/10.1080/00031305.2015.1090338 Implemented matching slope intercept using high breakdown regression estimator lmRob() package robust. Also introduces R functionality K distribution, alternative chisq distribution. particularly attractive teaching statistics; see vignette.
